response: Surface modifications on titanium implants, particularly rough-hydrophilic surfaces, skew macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory M2-like phenotype, characterized by higher arginase expression and IL-10 secretion, while hydrophobic surfaces promote a pro-inflammatory M1-like phenotype with high IL-1beta, IL-6, and TNFalpha secretion. In vivo, rough-hydrophilic implants in C57BL/6J mice reduced neutrophil presence and increased CD4+ and CD8+ T cells compared to smooth or rough implants. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice reduced pro-inflammatory macrophages and T cells, decreased pro-inflammatory cytokines, and increased bone-to-implant contact. Conversely, M1 macrophage transfer increased inflammatory cells and cytokines, resulting in the lowest osseointegration. Macrophage ablation increased neutrophils, reduced T cells and MSCs, and compromised bone formation, which could not be rescued by adoptive transfer of nave or polarized macrophages. In obese mice, rough-hydrophilic implants failed to induce anti-inflammatory polarization; instead, obesity shifted macrophage and T cell polarization toward pro-inflammatory phenotypes, with higher CD8+ T cells and lower Th2 and Th17 cells, reducing MSC recruitment and bone formation. Quantitative data: rough Ti implants had Sa=3.33 um, rough-hydrophilic Sa=3.31 um, smooth Sa=0.6 um; contact angles were 86deg, 0deg, and 62deg, respectively. Adoptive transfer used 1x10^6 cells/mouse. Contradictions: M1 macrophages worsened kidney injury in one model but were required for early fracture healing; macrophage ablation impaired healing, but heterogeneous macrophage populations were necessary for proper inflammatory resolution.
